title: Immanuel part 1
scripture_text: “The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and they will call him Immanuel (which means “God with us”).
devotional:
For you is life exhausting and full of great stress? Has the known and familiar, been replaced with uncertainty and insecurity? Has life become so difficult? Have you lost loved ones, or have you lost jobs and financial security?

Others who used to be fit and healthy are now no longer. Maybe your plans have been cancelled, hopes have been dashed and dreams shattered. And yet in the midst of all this exhaustion and pain of life we can have a wonderful, life enhancing hope.

A hope that we read about in the Christmas story. A hope that is a realistic, down to earth kind of hope, that enables us to cope with the harsh realities of the present.

The hope of the Christmas story is a hope that is not some kind of wishful thinking, it is not a hope that allows us to bury our heads in the sand, so we can pretend that the chaos and pain of our lives is not real. The glorious hope we read about in the Christmas story, and in the rest of the Gospels could be summed in one word. Emmanuel. ‘’She will give birth to a son, and you are to give him the name Jesus, because he will save his people from their sins.”All this took place to fulfil what the Lord had said through the prophet: The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and they will call him Immanuel (which means “God with us”).

The name Immanuel describes the essence of the heart of God.

It expresses God’s dreams and hopes.

It reveals God’s deepest desires. ‘God with us.’

In that name the purpose of creation is explained, ‘God with us.’

In that name the goal of salvation is discovered. ‘God with us’

In that name the will of God for our lives is disclosed, God with us.

Often, when we think of salvation we think in these terms. ‘God has saved me so

that when I die I will be able to go to heaven to be with Him.’ Now, that is absolutely true, however, there is an other truth that I think is even more important for the here and now, which is, ‘God has saved me so that he can be with me on earth.’

Immanuel sums up how our lives need to be defined in the here and now. If this is true then how can we relate to this God, and what difference will it make to the crazy, messy confusion of our lives?

a. We can relate to God and know that he is with us because of Jesus.

Before the angel told Joseph to call the baby Immanuel, he said ‘You shall call His name Jesus, Because he will save his people from their sins.’

Let me give you a personal illustration that might help us. Having a disability means that not everyone can relate to me, sometimes they don’t understand me, {I can’t think why!} At other times people feel awkward or embarrassed, so they avoid meaningful contact.

This is the kind of problem we all have with God. Our sin inhibits and limits our ability to know, enjoy and engage with God. We need to understand that the most serious consequence of our sin is that it prevents us from having intimacy with God.
prayer: Father thank that you as Immanuel, Jesus is able to relate to me and understand me.
html:
Immanuel part 1

Study Text: Matthew 1:23
“The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and they will call him Immanuel (which means “God with us”).

For you is life exhausting and full of great stress? Has the known and familiar, been replaced with uncertainty and insecurity? Has life become so difficult? Have you lost loved ones, or have you lost jobs and financial security?

Others who used to be fit and healthy are now no longer. Maybe your plans have been cancelled, hopes have been dashed and dreams shattered. And yet in the midst of all this exhaustion and pain of life we can have a wonderful, life enhancing hope.

A hope that we read about in the Christmas story. A hope that is a realistic, down to earth kind of hope, that enables us to cope with the harsh realities of the present.

The hope of the Christmas story is a hope that is not some kind of wishful thinking, it is not a hope that allows us to bury our heads in the sand, so we can pretend that the chaos and pain of our lives is not real. The glorious hope we read about in the Christmas story, and in the rest of the Gospels could be summed in one word. Emmanuel. ‘’She will give birth to a son, and you are to give him the name Jesus, because he will save his people from their sins.”All this took place to fulfil what the Lord had said through the prophet: The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and they will call him Immanuel (which means “God with us”).

The name Immanuel describes the essence of the heart of God.

It expresses God’s dreams and hopes.

It reveals God’s deepest desires. ‘God with us.’

In that name the purpose of creation is explained, ‘God with us.’

In that name the goal of salvation is discovered. ‘God with us’

In that name the will of God for our lives is disclosed, God with us.

Often, when we think of salvation we think in these terms. ‘God has saved me so

that when I die I will be able to go to heaven to be with Him.’ Now, that is absolutely true, however, there is an other truth that I think is even more important for the here and now, which is, ‘God has saved me so that he can be with me on earth.’

Immanuel sums up how our lives need to be defined in the here and now. If this is true then how can we relate to this God, and what difference will it make to the crazy, messy confusion of our lives?

a. We can relate to God and know that he is with us because of Jesus.

Before the angel told Joseph to call the baby Immanuel, he said ‘You shall call His name Jesus, Because he will save his people from their sins.’

Let me give you a personal illustration that might help us. Having a disability means that not everyone can relate to me, sometimes they don’t understand me, {I can’t think why!} At other times people feel awkward or embarrassed, so they avoid meaningful contact.

This is the kind of problem we all have with God. Our sin inhibits and limits our ability to know, enjoy and engage with God. We need to understand that the most serious consequence of our sin is that it prevents us from having intimacy with God.

Prayer: Father thank that you as Immanuel, Jesus is able to relate to me and understand me.
Bible in a year reading plan: - Job 22-24
